title: Design a Chat Messaging API
problemStatement: Design a RESTful API for a real-time chat application. Users can create conversations (1-on-1 or group), send messages, list conversations, and read message history. The API must handle read receipts and typing indicators.
functionalRequirements: Send a message to a conversation
List user's conversations with last message preview
Get message history for a conversation with pagination
constraints: Users must be members of a conversation to send/read messages
Message history should be paginated (cursor-based preferred)
Direct conversations between two users should be unique
Group conversations need at least 2 members

rubric.reasoning: Seven endpoints cover conversation lifecycle and messaging. Nested routes under /conversations/:id express clear ownership. Read receipts and member management are separate operations with distinct HTTP methods.

criteria: POST for creating, PUT for updates, DELETE for removal
rubric.reasoning: PUT for read receipts is correct — you're updating existing state (last read position), not creating a new resource. POST for messages creates a new message resource. DELETE for member removal is idempotent.
403 for non-members trying to read messages is critical — don't reveal message content. 409 for duplicate direct conversations tells the client to use the existing one instead of creating a new one.

Access control errors (not a member) must be handled at every endpoint that touches conversation data. For duplicate direct conversations, return 409 with the existing conversation ID so the client can redirect.
Every chat operation needs authentication — messages are private by nature. Even listing conversations requires knowing WHO is asking. No public endpoints in a messaging API.

Messages MUST use cursor-based pagination because new messages arrive constantly. Offset would cause duplicates. Conversations can use offset since they change less frequently. Cursor typically uses the last message's timestamp or ID.

criteria: Last message preview in conversation list
rubric.reasoning: Including lastMessage and unreadCount in the conversation list avoids N+1 queries — without them, the client would need to fetch each conversation's messages separately just to show the inbox.

defendQuestions.question: You designed a REST API for chat. How would you add real-time message delivery? Can REST handle this, or do you need WebSockets?
defendQuestions.modelReasoning: REST alone requires polling (client checks for new messages every N seconds). This wastes bandwidth and adds latency. WebSockets provide real-time push — the server sends messages to connected clients instantly. Best architecture: REST API for CRUD operations (create conversation, fetch history, mark read) + WebSocket connection for real-time events (new message, typing indicator, online status). REST for data management, WebSocket for live updates. This hybrid approach is what Slack, Discord, and WhatsApp use.

defendQuestions.question: In a group chat with 50 members, how do read receipts work at scale? Does each member generate a separate API call?
defendQuestions.modelReasoning: 50 members × every message = potentially thousands of read receipt updates. Solutions: (1) Batch read receipts — PUT /conversations/:id/read marks ALL messages up to a timestamp as read, not individual messages. (2) Send read receipts via WebSocket, not REST, to avoid HTTP overhead. (3) Don't store per-message read status — store per-user last_read_at timestamp and derive read status. (4) For large groups, show 'Read by 12 others' instead of individual names to reduce data.

defendQuestions.question: How do you guarantee messages appear in the correct order, especially when multiple users send messages at the same time?
defendQuestions.modelReasoning: Server-assigned timestamps (not client timestamps) determine order — clients have unreliable clocks. Use server UTC timestamp at insertion time. For concurrent messages (same millisecond), use a monotonically increasing ID as tiebreaker. Store messages with (conversation_id, created_at, message_id) and query with ORDER BY created_at ASC, id ASC. Client-side: insert messages based on server timestamp, re-sort if a delayed message arrives out of order.

endpoints.description: Get message history with cursor-based pagination
Send a message to a conversation
Mark messages as read up to a timestamp
Add a member to a group conversation
Remove a member from a group conversation

designNotes: Use cursor-based pagination for messages — offset pagination breaks when new messages arrive
Include lastMessage preview and unreadCount in conversation list for efficient UI rendering
Direct conversations should be unique per user pair — use 409 if already exists and return existing
Read receipts use PUT (updating state) not POST (not creating a resource)
